one_line_takeaway: Weak, inconsistent CL backdoor attacks (low/variable ASR) can still be repurposed as dataset-ownership watermarks by exploiting output-space density/similarity clustering rather than attack success rate, with BLTO and NA emerging as the most practical choices under a fidelity/verifiability/robustness trade-off, while SSL-Backdoor and CTRL work only on low-resolution data or specific architectures.
key_finding: Backdoor performance (ASR) at the encoder level is highly variable and often low: PoisonedEncoder and CorruptEncoder fail entirely (ASR ~10%, at random-baseline) once the target class exists in the pretraining set; SSL-Backdoor reaches only 13-39% ASR on CIFAR10 and nearly fails on ImageNet100; CTRL hits 90-98% ASR on SimCLR/SimSiam but collapses to ~10% on MoCo v2, and fails entirely on ImageNet100; BLTO consistently achieves 90.8-92.8% ASR on CIFAR10 and remains fairly strong (with ~10pp drop) on ImageNet100; NA reaches 88-100% ASR on CIFAR10 and 85-100% on ImageNet100. Despite these weak/unstable ASRs, the paper's core insight is that the same trigger-carrying samples still exhibit measurable output-space 'density concentration' (higher pairwise cosine similarity, Delta), which is a weaker but statistically usable signal than ASR for watermark verification. Verifiability results (Delta, x10^-2 scale, feature/soft-label/hard-label): SSL-Backdoor and CTRL work only on low-resolution CIFAR10 (e.g., SSL-Backdoor Delta up to 15.72 on SimCLR feature level) and fail almost completely on ImageNet100 (Delta near zero, indistinguishable from non-IP baselines); BLTO gives strong, consistent Delta across both datasets and all four architectures (e.g., 12.5-17.1 feature-level on CIFAR10, 21.65-23.85 feature-level on ImageNet100 downstream), though its hard-label signal is unreliable on ImageNet100; NA gives the largest Delta of any method (e.g., 54.51-83.86 x10^-2 on CIFAR10 feature/soft-label) but at the worst fidelity. Fidelity (Table II): on CIFAR10, CTRL and BLTO have SSIM > 0.95 (near-imperceptible) while NA has SSIM only 0.1232 (visually obtrusive due to patch-plus-stitching); on ImageNet100, SSL-Backdoor and CTRL achieve SSIM > 0.99 while NA remains poor (SSIM 0.2235) and BLTO degrades (SSIM 0.8254). Model utility (Table III) is largely preserved (encoder/downstream accuracy within ~1.5-2% of clean baseline for CIFAR10 and most ImageNet100 cases), except SSL-Backdoor on SimSiam+ImageNet100 causes a 4.12pp encoder / 8.16pp downstream accuracy drop. Robustness after fine-tuning on new downstream tasks (STL10, ImageNette): BLTO and NA remain robust or even strengthen post-fine-tuning; CTRL is fragile and loses effectiveness; SSL-Backdoor is only moderately robust at the soft-label level. Overall conclusion: BLTO offers the best fidelity/verifiability/robustness balance, NA offers maximal verifiability at the cost of visual fidelity, and SSL-Backdoor/CTRL are only viable under narrow conditions (low resolution, compatible architecture).
core_contribution: This is a JOURNAL OF LATEX CLASS FILES (submitted-style) manuscript with three own contributions, distinct from the works it evaluates/cites: (1) a unified re-evaluation of six existing data-poisoning-only CL backdoor attacks (SSL-Backdoor, PoisonedEncoder, CTRL, BLTO, CorruptEncoder, NA) across four CL model architectures (SimCLR, MoCo v2, BYOL, SimSiam) and two datasets (CIFAR10, ImageNet100), exposing generalization/robustness limitations not previously benchmarked in one place; (2) the paper's own novel idea -- repurposing four of those six attacks (SSL-Backdoor, CTRL, BLTO, NA) as dataset IP watermarking primitives, justified by the paper's own observation that trigger-carrying samples cluster with high 'density' in output space even when attack success rate (ASR) is low; and (3) a new multi-level (feature / soft-label / hard-label) black-box verification scheme built on a similarity-difference statistic Delta = S' - S (mean pairwise cosine similarity of watermarked vs. clean query outputs) combined with a one-sample t-test hypothesis-testing framework (H0: Delta_suspect <= tau) for ownership verification, evaluated along three self-defined axes: fidelity, verifiability, and robustness.
threat_model: Two-party threat model defined in Section IV-A: (1) Pretraining Dataset Owner -- the IP owner, who before public release injects a small set of watermark (backdoor-triggered) samples from a known target class into an otherwise unlabeled CL dataset, and later performs black-box ownership verification purely via API queries (feature vectors, soft labels, or hard labels), without access to the suspect model's parameters or training process. (2) Model Developer -- the party that scrapes/procures the (possibly watermarked) dataset, trains a CL encoder on it via standard procedures, and is assumed unaware the data is watermarked; they may expose the encoder or a downstream classifier via API, commercially. The dataset owner does not control or intervene in training/loss functions -- this is the key distinction the paper draws against prior model-level CL watermarking schemes (e.g., SSLGuard, SSL-WM) that assume training-pipeline control, which the paper argues is impractical for dataset (rather than model) IP protection.
limitations: The paper's own stated/implied limitations: (1) PoisonedEncoder and CorruptEncoder are declared fundamentally unsuitable for this repurposing because their backdoor mechanism assumes the attacker's target class is absent from the pretraining set -- an assumption incompatible with a dataset-owner watermarking use case where the target class is drawn from within the owner's own dataset; only 4 of the original 6 attacks survive as usable watermarks. (2) Hard-label-level verification is repeatedly shown to be unreliable/inconsistent across all four surviving methods (e.g., BLTO and NA both show negative or near-zero Delta at the hard-label level in several architecture/dataset combinations), meaning the scheme's applicability shrinks when only hard-label API access is available. (3) Verifiability is architecture- and resolution-dependent: CTRL fails completely on MoCo v2 and on ImageNet100; SSL-Backdoor fails almost completely on ImageNet100 due to fixed patch size becoming proportionally tiny on high-resolution images. (4) There is an explicit fidelity-verifiability trade-off with no single dominant method: NA (best verifiability) has the worst perceptual quality (SSIM as low as 0.1232), making it detectable/removable by a defender inspecting the dataset, while BLTO and CTRL (best fidelity) have narrower or architecture-limited verifiability. (5) The optimal decision threshold tau for the hypothesis test is shown to vary by method, dataset, and verification level, and the paper notes a dataset owner realistically does not know the suspect model's exact architecture, forcing aggregation across architectures which can blunt TPR/FPR precision. (6) All experiments are confined to image-only CL (SimCLR, MoCo v2, BYOL, SimSiam) on CIFAR10/ImageNet100/STL10/ImageNette; the paper explicitly excludes multimodal (image-text) CL backdoors such as Carlini et al.'s poisoned image-text pairs, so generalization to CLIP-style or text-modality watermarking is untested. (7) No adaptive-adversary evaluation is presented -- the paper does not test whether a model developer aware of this watermarking scheme could detect/strip the watermark or evade verification (e.g., via fine-tuning to specifically erase clustering signals, or dataset filtering/deduplication defenses).

quantitative_results: SSL-Backdoor ASR (CIFAR10, encoder level): ~13% on MoCo v2 to ~39% on BYOL
PoisonedEncoder ASR: never exceeds random baseline (~10%) on any architecture (CIFAR10)
CTRL ASR (CIFAR10): 97.9% on SimCLR, 93.2% on SimSiam, ~66% on BYOL, ~10% (random) on MoCo v2; fails almost completely on ImageNet100
BLTO ASR (CIFAR10): 90.8% (SimCLR) to 92.8% (BYOL), converging after 500 epochs; on ImageNet100 drops ~10pp but remains relatively high
CorruptEncoder ASR: stays around 10% (random baseline) on all models (CIFAR10)
NA ASR: exceeds 88% on all CIFAR10 models, near 100% on BYOL and SimCLR; on ImageNet100 near 100% on SimCLR/BYOL/SimSiam, ~85% on MoCo v2
Clean/backdoored encoder accuracy (CIFAR10, 600 epochs): all methods land between 75% and 85%, mostly ~80%, no significant utility loss

Fidelity Table II (CIFAR10): SSL-Backdoor SSIM 0.8815/LPIPS 0.2173/DreamSim 0.2920; CTRL SSIM 0.9537/LPIPS 0.0933/DreamSim 0.1013; BLTO SSIM 0.9526/LPIPS 0.0774/DreamSim 0.1038; NA SSIM 0.1232/LPIPS 0.5664/DreamSim 0.4255
Fidelity Table II (ImageNet100): SSL-Backdoor SSIM 0.9913/LPIPS 0.0216; CTRL SSIM 0.9928/LPIPS 5.83e-5; BLTO SSIM 0.8254/LPIPS 0.1243; NA SSIM 0.2235/LPIPS 0.6494
Model utility drop (Table III): SSL-Backdoor on SimSiam+ImageNet100 -- encoder accuracy 43.26% (clean) to 39.14% (watermarked), a 4.12pp drop; downstream accuracy 48.62% to 40.46%, an 8.16pp drop

Verifiability Delta (x10^-2, feature level, CIFAR10 IP vs baseline): SSL-Backdoor up to 15.72 (SimCLR); CTRL up to 31.49 (SimCLR) but fails on MoCo v2 (0.74); BLTO 12.19-17.11 across architectures; NA 13.56-80.28 (largest among all methods)
Verifiability Delta on ImageNet100 (feature level): SSL-Backdoor and CTRL near zero (fail); BLTO 21.65-23.85 (downstream fine-tuned); NA 5.15-10.32 (pretraining) and much higher post-fine-tuning

Hypothesis testing (16 suspect models per setting: 4 IP-trained, 12 non-IP): on CIFAR10, BLTO and NA maintain perfect TPR at feature/soft-label levels up to threshold ~0.14-0.2 with FPR near zero; on ImageNet100, SSL-Backdoor and CTRL TPR drops to zero at any positive threshold, while BLTO/NA remain effective at feature level up to threshold ~0.12-0.18
baselines_compared: Clean (no watermark) CL training as fidelity/utility baseline
STL10 and ImageNette used as non-IP (clean) comparison datasets for false-positive testing
